summary: On September 11, 2001, I was at Ellis Island when the attacks happened. I went out to the Ellis Island fuel dock with my secretary to witness the burning World Trade Center towers and took pictures. We organized the staff on the island, set up triage and a morgue, and helped incoming victims and rescue workers. The island became a critical support site during the crisis, and I stayed there through the night managing the situation.

I witnessed the World Trade Center towers being hit and burning from Ellis Island on 9/11. The first tower was burning when I arrived, and shortly after, I saw the second plane hit the second tower. Later, both buildings collapsed, which was shocking and unexpected for everyone watching. The destruction of the World Trade Center was the central event that triggered the emergency response we coordinated from Ellis Island.

The September 11 attacks were a shocking and tragic event that I experienced firsthand from Ellis Island. Initially, we thought a small plane had accidentally crashed, but after the second plane hit, it was clear this was an intentional attack. We mobilized quickly to assist victims, set up triage and morgue facilities, and coordinated with rescue teams from various places. The emotional impact was profound, with people praying, crying, and struggling to grasp the magnitude of what was happening.

Doug Porter was the engineer from the Denver Service Center who was with me on Ellis Island during the 9/11 attacks. We stood together by the flagpole discussing the situation as the first World Trade Center building collapsed. Doug was part of the team mobilizing to manage the crisis on the island, and we worked closely during those intense moments.

The Denver Service Center was represented on Ellis Island during the 9/11 attacks by Doug Porter, their engineer. Doug and I worked together to organize the response efforts on the island as the events unfolded. The center’s involvement was part of the broader National Park Service support during the crisis.

The New York City Police Department played a vital role during the 9/11 attacks by bringing victims to Ellis Island via boats. I saw NYPD boats landing on the Ellis Island slip, unloading injured people including firemen and others with severe injuries. Their quick response was crucial in getting victims to triage and medical assistance set up on the island.

During the 9/11 response at Ellis Island, I met rescue and EMT personnel who had come from Connecticut to assist with triage and the morgue. About 50 or 60 of these responders arrived to help manage the overwhelming number of victims. Their support was invaluable in the chaotic hours following the attacks.

Along with responders from Connecticut, EMTs and rescue workers from Massachusetts came to Ellis Island to help after the 9/11 attacks. They assisted with setting up and running the triage and morgue facilities on the island. Their presence highlighted the regional cooperation during this unprecedented emergency.

I live on Staten Island and on 9/11, I couldn’t get home because of the emergency. I stayed on Ellis Island through the night with my deputy chief of maintenance. Staten Island was essentially cut off, and many others who lived there were stuck as well. It was a surreal experience being unable to return home during such a crisis.

Manhattan was the site of the World Trade Center attacks that I witnessed from Ellis Island. After the attacks, I went over to Manhattan with a crew on a Park Service work vessel called Liberty II to assist, but the area was already crowded with responders. Later that night, I returned to Manhattan to check on Federal Hall and address a flood at one of the sites, showing how the emergency response extended into the city itself.

On the night of September 11, after staying on Ellis Island, I went to Federal Hall National Memorial in Manhattan to check on the site. We found the cooling tower was overflowing and shut down the gas and utilities to prevent further damage. It was part of the broader effort to secure important landmarks in the city during the crisis.

Liberty II is a 65-foot Park Service work vessel that I used on September 11 to go over to Manhattan with EMTs and a captain to assist with the emergency. However, the area was already crowded with boats and responders, so I decided to return to Ellis Island to continue our efforts there. Liberty II was an important resource for transportation and support during the crisis.

New York City was at the center of the September 11 attacks, and I was involved in the emergency response from Ellis Island. We coordinated evacuations, set up triage and morgue facilities, and assisted victims brought over by boats from the city. The scale of the tragedy was overwhelming, and the city’s agencies, including the NYPD and fire department, worked tirelessly alongside us to manage the crisis.
